26: poreutheti pros ton laon touton kai eipon (poreuthēti pros ton laon touton kai eipon): Paul's final quotation of Isaiah 6:9-10 to the Roman Jewish community. This is the third time Acts invokes the Isaianic hardening: Jesus used it in Luke 8:10; Acts 28 closes the narrative with it. The pattern is the 'to you and to them' logic: the gospel comes first to the Jew (Romans 1:16 priority) and when resisted turns outward. Luke does not say Israel is abandoned — Paul is still quoting Scripture to Jews — but the structural conclusion of Acts is the Gentile mission unhindered (akōlytōs, the book's last word).
1: 'Once safely on shore, we found out that the island was called Malta' — diasōthentes (thoroughly saved/completely rescued — dia + sōzō, the salvation-verb) — the same verb root as Paul's promise 'you will be saved' (Acts 28:30, 31, 44). Luke's use of diasōzō at the moment of arrival on shore makes the landing a salvation-event: the promise of Acts 28:22-24 has been exactly fulfilled. Malta (Melitē — honey, from the Phoenician) was a Carthaginian and later Roman colony, strategically located in the central Mediterranean; its population was Punic-speaking, not Greek.
2: 'The islanders showed us unusual kindness (philanthropian — philanthropy/human-kindness). They built a fire and welcomed us all because it was raining and cold' — philanthropia (disp=0 but significant: the same root as Julius's philanthropōs in Acts 27:3) is Luke's summary of Malta's reception: the pagan islanders demonstrate the human-kindness that the Roman centurion demonstrated — and that the Jerusalem mob failed to demonstrate. The fire (pyran — a bonfire), the welcome of 276 wet, cold, exhausted shipwreck survivors, is the practical form of the philanthropia. Luke repeatedly notes when pagan generosity exceeds religious community's self-protection.
3: 'Paul gathered a pile of brushwood and, as he put it on the fire, a viper, driven out by the heat, fastened itself on his hand' — Paul's participation in gathering firewood (phryganōn ti plēthos synstrephantos — gathering a multitude of sticks) is the apostolic pattern: he does not stand apart from manual labor (cf. Acts 18:3; Acts 20:34-35). The viper (echidna — a venomous snake) driven out by the fire's heat (apo tēs thermēs — from the heat) is a realistic detail; the creature was in the brushwood, seeking warmth, and biting defensively when disturbed. 'Fastened itself on his hand' (kathapsen tēs cheiros autou — seized/bit his hand) — the hold was enough to hang from his hand (Acts 28:4 — hanging from his hand).
4: 'When the islanders saw the snake hanging from his hand, they said to each other: This man must be a murderer; for though he escaped from the sea, Justice (Dikē) has not allowed him to live' — Dikē (Justice, Divine Justice) is the Greek goddess of retributive justice — the Maltese invoke her as the cosmic principle that punishes guilt even when human courts fail. The theological assumption is exact: the sea's survival proves innocence of sea-related sins; the snakebite suggests a guilt-beyond-survival. The Maltese reasoning is not ignorant superstition but follows a coherent Greek theological framework in which the gods close every escape route for the guilty.
5: 'But Paul shook the snake off into the fire and suffered no ill effects' — apotinaxas to thrion eis to pyr (shaking off the creature into the fire) — the casual, unhurried gesture ('shook off') rather than a panicked reaction suggests either divine calmness or divine protection; the narrative implies both. 'Suffered no ill effects' (ouden kakon epathen — he suffered nothing bad) — the Maltese expected swelling (prēsthai — to swell, Acts 28:6) and collapse. Neither came. The fulfillment of the snake-immunity promise (Luke 10:19 — 'I have given you authority to tread on serpents') enacted on Malta.
6: 'The people expected him to swell up or suddenly fall dead (nekron — dead; disp=2); but after waiting a long time and seeing nothing unusual happen to him, they changed their minds and said he was a god' — nekron (dead; disp=2) — the ordinary sense of a corpse, not the resurrection-technical sense. The Maltese theological inversion is complete: murderer → god; the same evidence (snake-bite + survival) that first confirmed his guilt now confirms his divinity. The crowd's oscillation mirrors the Lystra crowd's progression from 'the gods have come down to us' (Acts 14:11) to stoning (Acts 14:19) — pagan theological attribution is unstable. Luke records the divine-attribution without correcting it in this scene (contrast Acts 14:14-15 where Paul and Barnabas refuse worship).
7: 'There was an estate nearby that belonged to Publius, the chief official (prōtō — first, leading official) of the island. He welcomed us to his home and showed us generous hospitality for three days' — 'Prōtos tēs nēsou' (the First of the island) is confirmed as an actual title by two Maltese inscriptions using exactly this Greek term. Luke's use of the official title is another authenticity marker: he records the correct administrative designation for Malta's Roman governor. Three days of generous hospitality (philophronōs — with lover-of-strangers kindness, philanthropy's sibling virtue) for 276 shipwreck survivors is a substantial logistical commitment.
8: 'His father (patēr — father; disp=2) was sick in bed, suffering from fever and dysentery. Paul went in to see him and, after prayer, placed his hands on him and healed him' — patēr (father; disp=2) in the domestic sense — Publius's biological father. The fever (pyretois — fevers, plural — indicating recurring or multiple fevers) combined with dysentery (dysenteria — bloody flux) is consistent with Malta Fever (brucellosis), endemic to Malta from unpasteurized goat's milk. 'After prayer' (proseuxamenos — having prayed) distinguishes Paul's healing from medical practice or magical technique: prayer acknowledges the divine source of the healing; 'placed his hands on him' is the physical channel; healing follows.
9: 'When this had happened, the rest of those on the island who had diseases came and were cured' — the healing of Publius's father becomes the occasion for a general healing mission: 'the rest of those who had diseases' (hoi loipoi hoi en tē nēsō echontes asthenias — the remaining ones on the island having illnesses) came to Paul. The pattern repeats: one high-status healing opens a community-wide healing mission (cf. Peter's mother-in-law healing → whole city gathers at sunset, Mark 1:32-34; Luke 4:40-41). Malta receives a ministry of healing that parallels the preaching-healing combination Paul conducted in cities throughout the journeys.
10: 'They honoured us in many ways; and when we were ready to sail, they furnished us with the supplies we needed' — 'honoured us in many ways' (pollais timais etīmēsan hēmas — they honoured us with many honours) — timē (honour, value) can refer to honorific recognition or to monetary compensation/payment; the latter is possible here (they paid them for the healing ministry). 'Furnished us with the supplies we needed' (tois pros tas chreias — the things toward the needs) — practical provisioning for the voyage continuation. The Malta interlude demonstrates the gospel's power to generate community (receiving, healing, mutual supply) even without a church established.
11: 'After three months we put out to sea in a ship that had wintered in the island — it was an Alexandrian ship with the figurehead of the twin gods Castor and Pollux' — the three months on Malta (October/November through January/February) is the closed-navigation period. The Alexandrian ship (another grain ship from the Egypt-Italy route, wintering at Malta) bears the figurehead Dioskouroi (Castor and Pollux — the twin sons of Zeus, patron deities of sailors, the constellation Gemini). The pagans on the ship travel under the protection of their sea-gods; Luke notes the figurehead without comment, letting the journey's safety under Paul's God speak for itself.
12: 'We put in at Syracuse and stayed there three days' — Syracuse (Syrakousas — the major Greek city on Sicily's SE coast) was the most important city in Sicily and a major port on the Italy-bound route. Three days there provided rest, provisioning, and perhaps contact with the local community. Luke's route-summary (Syracuse → Rhegium → Puteoli → Appian Way → Rome) is the standard Italy-arrival itinerary for Mediterranean ships and can be traced on a map with precision.
13: 'From there we set sail and arrived at Rhegium. The next day the south wind came up, and on the following day we reached Puteoli' — Rhegium (modern Reggio di Calabria) is at the toe of Italy, across the Strait of Messina from Sicily. The southerly (notos — south wind) that came up the next day at Rhegium enabled a fast run up Italy's west coast to Puteoli (modern Pozzuoli, near Naples), the main Italian port for Alexandrian grain ships. The two-day run from Rhegium to Puteoli is consistent with favorable southerly winds through the Strait of Messina.
14: 'There we found some brothers and sisters who invited us to spend a week with them. And so we came to Rome' — the Christians at Puteoli (not otherwise mentioned in the NT) represent the first Roman Christians Paul encounters: the gospel reached Italy before Paul did, through the network of travelers and traders. The seven-day stay (epimeinai par' autois hēmeras hepta — to remain with them seven days) required Julius's permission — he continues to treat Paul with the philanthropia established at Sidon. 'And so we came to Rome' — Luke's matter-of-fact announcement of the journey's end: Rome, the goal since Acts 19:21.
15: 'The brothers and sisters there had heard that we were coming, and they travelled as far as the Forum of Appius and the Three Taverns to meet us. At the sight of these people Paul thanked God and was encouraged' — Roman Christians (not yet met by Paul) came 40+ miles (Forum of Appius on the Appian Way) and 30+ miles (Three Taverns, further toward Rome) to meet him. The coming-to-meet (eis apantēsin — for a meeting, for an encounter; this is the technical term for the formal civic reception of a dignitary — the parousia-reception formula) mirrors the language of 1 Thessalonians 4:17 ('to meet the Lord in the air'). Paul's encouragers are the ones who came to escort him in to Rome. 'Thanked God and was encouraged' — the two responses to divine faithfulness: gratitude and renewed courage.
16: 'When we got to Rome, Paul was allowed to live by himself, with a soldier to guard him' — 'live by himself' (menein kath' heauton — to remain by himself, privately) — Paul is not imprisoned in the public jail but lives in his own rented accommodation (Acts 28:30 — en idiō misthōmati — in his own hired dwelling) under house arrest, with a single military guard. This custodia militaris (military custody) allowed him to receive visitors, write letters, and continue preaching — the form of imprisonment that produced Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, and Philemon.
17: 'Three days later he called together the local Jewish leaders. When they had assembled, Paul said to them: My brothers, although I have done nothing against our people or against the customs of our ancestors, I was arrested in Jerusalem and handed over to the Romans' — Paul's immediate initiative toward the Roman Jewish community (synagogē archous — the synagogue leaders, the plural implies multiple Roman synagogues) follows his invariable pattern: to the Jew first. 'My brothers' (andres adelphoi — men, brothers) is Paul's standard synagogue address (Acts 13:15; Acts 22:1; Acts 23:1, 6; Acts 28:17). His defense is pre-emptive: he establishes innocence (nothing against the people or the ancestral customs) before any accusation arrives.
18: 'They examined me and wanted to release me, because I was not guilty of any crime deserving death' — Paul's summary of the Roman judicial process (Felix, Festus, Agrippa — Acts 24-26) in one sentence: the consistent conclusion of three Roman officials was innocence (cf. Acts 23:29 — Claudius Lysias; Acts 25:25 — Festus; Acts 26:31-32 — Agrippa). Luke has documented the legal innocence of Paul at length so that Paul's arrival at Rome as a prisoner cannot be misread as evidence of guilt; it is evidence of Jewish political pressure, not Roman legal condemnation.
19: 'When the Jews objected, I was compelled to make an appeal to Caesar. I certainly did not intend to bring any charge against my own nation' — Paul's appeal to Caesar (Acts 25:11) was defensive, not offensive: it was not to accuse the Jews before Caesar but to prevent the Jerusalem Sanhedrin from acquiring jurisdiction over his case. 'I certainly did not intend to bring any charge' (ōs tou ethnous mou echōn ti katēgorein — not as having anything to accuse my nation of) — the syntax of denial; Paul approaches Rome's Jews as a fellow Jew, not as an adversary.
20: 'For this reason I have asked to see you and talk with you. It is because of the hope of Israel (tēn elpida tou Israēl — the hope of Israel; disp=2 for elpis) that I am bound with this chain' — elpis tou Israēl (the hope of Israel) is the theological summary of Paul's entire mission: the hope = the resurrection (Acts 23:6; Acts 24:15; Acts 26:6-7), which is Israel's covenantal expectation. Paul's chain is not a Roman punishment for crime but a paradoxical badge of fidelity to Israel's own hope. The chain that binds him binds him to the promise that bound Abraham.
21: 'They replied: We have not received any letters from Judea concerning you, and none of our people who have come from there has reported or said anything bad about you' — the Roman Jewish community's ignorance of Paul is surprising given his prominence in Jerusalem's temple riots (Acts 21:27-36); either the Jerusalem leadership chose not to pursue the matter to Rome, or communications had not yet arrived. The Romans' openness (Acts 28:22 — 'we want to hear what your views are') gives Paul the opportunity he has had in every city: the receptive audience that precedes the decisive division.
22: 'But we want to hear what your views are, for we know that people everywhere are talking against this sect (haireseōs tautēs — this sect/choice/party)' — hairesis (sect, school of thought, disp=0 here but theologically significant) was the standard Greek term for a philosophical or religious movement defined by distinctive doctrines. The same word is used for the 'sect of the Nazarenes' (Acts 24:5) and Paul's own self-description of 'the Way which they call a sect' (Acts 24:14). The Roman Jews' description ('people everywhere are talking against this sect') confirms the movement's wide, if controversial, recognition.
23: 'They arranged to meet Paul on a certain day, and came in even larger numbers to the place where he was staying. He witnessed to them from morning till evening, explaining and testifying about the kingdom of God, and trying to persuade them about Jesus from the Law of Moses (nomou Mōyseōs — the Law of Moses; disp=3 for nomos) and the Prophets' — the 'morning till evening' exposition is the longest single preaching session recorded in Acts — Paul's full-day messianic demonstration from Torah and Prophets. The two-witness structure (Law of Moses + Prophets) is the standard prophetic-fulfillment argument (Luke 24:27, 44). The kingdom of God (Acts 28:23, 31) bookends the chapter: Acts begins with the disciples asking about the kingdom (Acts 1:3) and ends with Paul proclaiming it.
24: 'Some were convinced by what he said, but others would not believe' — the divided response (hoi men... hoi de — some... others) is the universal pattern of Paul's synagogue missions: Antioch (Acts 13:43-45), Iconium (Acts 14:1-2), Thessalonica (Acts 17:4-5), Berea (Acts 17:11-12), Corinth (Acts 18:4-8). The division is not accidental; it is the predicted consequence of the Isaiah word that Paul is about to quote. The division between believing Jews and unbelieving Jews does not end Jewish mission — it explains it theologically.
25: 'They disagreed among themselves and began to leave after Paul had made this final statement: The Holy Spirit (to pneuma to hagion — the Holy Spirit; disp=3 for pneuma, disp=2 for hagios) spoke the truth to your ancestors (dia Ēsaiou tou prophētou — through Isaiah the prophet) when he said' — Paul's attribution of Isaiah 6:9-10 to 'the Holy Spirit' makes the Spirit the author of OT prophecy: the prophetic word is simultaneously Isaiah's word and the Spirit's word. 'Your ancestors' (pateras hymōn — your fathers; disp=2 for patēr — the OT-covenant fathers to whom Isaiah originally spoke) — the quotation applies to both the original audience and their descendants who now stand before Paul.
27: 'For this people's heart has become calloused; they hardly hear with their ears, and they have closed their eyes. Otherwise they might see with their eyes, hear with their ears, understand with their hearts and turn, and I would heal them' — the Isaiah 6:9-10 citation (in its LXX form) is the most-quoted OT passage in the NT: Matthew (Acts 13:14-15), Mark (Acts 4:12), Luke (Acts 8:10), John (Acts 12:40), and now Acts 28:26-27 all apply it to Jewish unbelief. The LXX version emphasizes the people's own self-blinding ('they have closed their eyes' — ekammusan — aorist active, they closed) rather than the divine hardening of the Hebrew ('make their hearts fat'). The difference matters: the Greek version leaves more room for the culpability of the people who chose not to see.
28: 'Therefore I want you to know that God's salvation (to sōtērion tou theou — the salvation of God; disp=2 for theos) has been sent to the Gentiles, and they will listen!' — to sōtērion (the salvation, used as a noun) echoes the Nunc Dimittis (Luke 2:30 — 'for my eyes have seen your salvation') and Simeon's identification of Jesus as the sōtērion prepared before all peoples. Paul's final declaration to Rome's Jewish leaders repeats what has been said since Acts 13:46: the Gentile turn is a consequence of Jewish unbelief, not a replacement of Jewish priority. 'They will listen' (autoi kai akousontai — they indeed will hear/listen) — the Gentile receptivity that Isaiah's hardened-Israel passage implies by contrast.
29: 'After he said this, the Jews left, arguing vigorously among themselves' — verse 29 is omitted in most early MSS (it does not appear in p74, Aleph, A, B, E, or 33); it is present in later MSS and the Byzantine tradition. Many commentators consider it a scribal addition clarifying that the Jews departed. Whether original or not, its content is consistent with the narrative pattern: the divided-and-departing response (hoi men... hoi de — some... others, as in Acts 28:24) has occurred at every Paul-synagogue encounter; the argument among themselves is the community's internal division that the gospel always produces.
30: 'For two whole years Paul stayed there in his own rented house and welcomed all who came to see him' — the 'whole years' (holēn dietian — an entire two-year period) at Rome in custodia militaris produced the prison letters (Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, Philemon) and the ongoing mission summarized in Acts 28:31. 'In his own rented house' (en idiō misthōmati — at his own expense) — Paul paid for his own lodging even under arrest; the manual labor and tent-making trade sustained him even in Rome. 'Welcomed all who came to see him' — the open-door policy of Paul's imprisonment: the chain does not imprison the gospel.
31: 'He proclaimed (kērassōn — heralding, preaching) the kingdom of God and taught about the Lord Jesus Christ — with all boldness (meta pasēs parrēsias — with all openness/freedom of speech) and without hindrance (akōlytōs — unhindered, prevented-by-no-one; the final word of the book)' — the book of Acts ends on akōlytōs — a single adverb meaning 'without hindrance, unimpeded.' The gospel has traveled from Jerusalem to Rome without final suppression; Paul preaches the kingdom and teaches the Lord Jesus with full parrēsia (bold free-speech) in the capital of the empire. The open ending — no mention of Paul's trial outcome, no resolution — is Luke's deliberate conclusion: the word of God is not bound (2 Timothy 2:9); the story continues beyond any document's last page.
